title: Toby Philpott
content:
{{Top|rwp|ffa}}
{{Person
|image=[[File:Infobox_philpott.jpg]]
|name=Toby Philpott
|birth=[[February 14]], [[1940s|1946]], [[Wikipedia:London|London]], [[Wikipedia:England|England]]<ref name="Philpott Page">{{WebCite|url=http://homepage.ntlworld.com/toby.p/index.html|text=Toby Philpott|work=Toby Philpott Official Website|archivedate=20140502221221}}</ref>
|death=
|listedpronouns=<!--Only include pronouns if they have been explicitly specified by the individual to which this article pertains.-->
|nationality=[[Wikipedia:England|English]]<ref name="Philpott Page" />
|occupation=*[[Wikipedia:Archaeology|Archeologist]]<ref name="Philpott Page" />
*[[Wikipedia:Clown|Clown]]<ref name="Philpott Page" />
*[[Wikipedia:Juggling|Juggler]]<ref name="Philpott Page" />
*[[Wikipedia:Library technician|Library technician]]<ref name="Philpott Page" />
*[[Wikipedia:Puppeteer|Puppeteer]]<ref name="Philpott Page" />
*[[Wikipedia:Teacher|Teacher]]<ref name="Philpott Page" />

|companies=

'''Toby Philpott''' is an [[Wikipedia:England|English]] [[Wikipedia:Puppeteer|puppeteer]] best known for his work in [[Wikipedia:Film|motion picture]] [[Wikipedia:Animatronics|animatronics]] during the 1980s in such films as [[Wikipedia:The Dark Crystal|''The Dark Crystal'']] and [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']]. Born into a family of [[Wikipedia:Entertainment|entertainers]], Philpott dropped out of school and traveled the world during the [[1960s]], [[Wikipedia:Squatting|squatting]] in various locations and surviving off money he earned from his work as a [[Wikipedia:Busking|street performer]], which included [[Wikipedia:Juggling|juggling]], [[Wikipedia:Fire eating|fire eating]], [[Wikipedia:Magic (illusion)|magic shows]], [[Wikipedia:Clown|clowning]], and [[Wikipedia:Acrobatics|acrobatics]]. He began his film career after [[Wikipedia:Jim Henson|Jim Henson]] personally selected Philpott to work on the groundbreaking [[1982]] [[Wikipedia:Fantasy film|fantasy film]] ''The Dark Crystal'', in which he worked side-by-side with Henson.

The next year, Philpott was approached to serve as one of the puppeteers controlling [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba Desilijic Tiure]] in ''Return of the Jedi''. Philpott controlled the left arm, head, tongue, and body of the giant [[Hutt/Legends|Hutt]] puppet. Philpott would lend his [[Wikipedia:Puppetry|puppetry]] skills to other such movies as [[Wikipedia:The Company of Wolves|''The Company of Wolves'']] ([[1984]]), [[Wikipedia:Labyrinth (film)|''Labyrinth'']] ([[1986]]), [[Wikipedia:Little Shop of Horrors (film)|''Little Shop of Horrors'']] (1986) and [[Wikipedia:Who Framed Roger Rabbit|''Who Framed Roger Rabbit'']] ([[1988]]) before leaving the film industry. He currently works as a [[Wikipedia:Library technician|library technician]] at the Cardiff Central Library in [[Wikipedia:Cardiff|Cardiff]], [[Wikipedia:Wales|Wales]]. Reflecting upon his movie career, Philpott describes himself as a "street juggler that got lucky."<ref name="Blue Harvest" />

==Biography==
===''Return of the Jedi''===
{{Quote|It was physically hard work, but the sculpt was so wonderful that animating Jabba was a joy.|Toby Philpott|<ref name="Habidabad">{{WebCite|url=http://habidabad.com/toby_interview.htm|text=Toby Philpott Interview|author=Habidabad|work=The Book of Habidabad|archivedate=20111003004448}}</ref>}}
While working on [[Wikipedia:The Dark Crystal|''The Dark Crystal'']] at [[Wikipedia:Jim Henson|Jim Henson's]] [[Wikipedia:Jim Henson's Creature Shop|Creature Shop]], rumors began to circulate that [[Alien/Legends|alien]] characters for [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']] were being developed at that same studio, and several of the puppeteers began looking for ways to get a job on the film. [[David Barclay]], who had been a puppet builder for ''The Dark Crystal'', was chosen as one of the operators of the massive [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]] puppet, and asked for Toby Philpott to be hired as his partner. Philpott, who was unaware of Barclay's politicking on his behalf, was surprised by the sudden job offer and eagerly accepted it.<ref name="Habidabad" />

[[File:Philpott_jabba.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Toby Philpott working inside the [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba]] puppet on the set of [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']].]]
Barclay was the chief Jabba puppeteer who planned all the movements and guided all the other performers; Philpott controlled Jabba's left arm, with which he controlled many of Jabba's more active motions, including eating [[Klatooine paddy frog/Legends|Klatooine paddy frogs]], smoking the [[Hookah pipe/Legends|Hookah pipe]], and physically assaulting [[Bib Fortuna/Legends|Bib Fortuna]] and [[C-3PO/Legends|C-3PO]]. Philpott also used his right hand to control Jabba's tongue and animate his head, and controlled the body by swiveling his seat with his legs braces. Cable controls allowed Philpott to swivel the head and snarl Jabba's Mouth.<ref name="From Star Wars to Jedi">[[From Star Wars to Jedi: The Making of a Saga]]</ref>

The rest of the puppet team included Barclay on the right arm and mouth; [[Mike Edmonds]] on the tail; and [[John Coppinger]], who controlled the eyes via [[Wikipedia:Remote control|remote control]] and gave the others feedback based on what he saw from the outside.<ref name="T-Bone">{{WebCite|url=http://www.starwarz.com/tbone/index.php?categoryid=20&p2_articleid=127|text=Interview with Toby Philpott|author=T-bone|date=2003-07-31|work=T'bone's Star Wars Universe|archivedate=20080930233014}}</ref> Philpott would later describe sitting inside the Jabba puppet with Barclay as being "like a two-man submarine" because it was very hot, cramped, and uncomfortable.<ref name="Blue Harvest">{{WebCite|url=http://www.blueharvest.net/scoops/toby.shtml|text=Chatting with Jabba the Hutt|work=BlueHarvest.net|archivedate=20081118195253}}</ref> A monitor inside the puppet shows a general shot of Jabba that helped the puppeteers operate, although they could not tell where the frame of the shot was from the image; the team depended on feedback amongst themselves, and their training under Jim Henson gave them experience in coordinating to create a single creature.<ref name="T-Bone" />

Philpott and Barclay would go inside the puppet early in the day and remain there for almost the entire nine-and-one-half hours of filming each day,<ref name="Lightsabre">{{WebCite|url=http://www.lightsabre.co.uk/Interviews/tobyphilpottinterview.htm|text=The Lightsabre Interview: Toby Philpott|work=Lightsabre|archivedate=20081008044548}}</ref> so much so that other cast and crew members did not know who they were during breaks. The two would only occasionally leave the suit to directly discuss Jabba-related business, but preferred to remain in character within the suit. Philpott and Barclay employed an approach by Jim Henson in which they took direction while in character; director [[Richard Marquand]] agreed to give Jabba direction as if the character was the actor, and they talked back to the director as if the puppet itself were a live performer.<ref name="Blue Harvest" />

It took about three weeks to film the [[Jabba's Palace/Legends|Jabba's Palace]] scenes, and an additional week to film the [[Khetanna/Legends|Sail Barge]] scenes; Philpott and the puppeteers said they experienced few problems operating Jabba due to the effective design work of [[Phil Tippett]], [[Stuart Freeborn]], and [[John Coppinger]].<ref name="Tosche Station">{{WebCite|url=http://www.toschestation.nl/interviews/interview-tph.php|text=Interview: Tody Philpott - Jabba the Hutt|work=toschestation.nl|language=Dutch|archivedate=20080930191526}}</ref> Philpott's previous experience with ''The Dark Crystal'' helped prepare Philpott for the role of Jabba, although as a result, he later said he wasn't as "overawed" by the experience as he might have been. Nevertheless, he described the experience as "great fun" and said he enjoyed being the center of attention among the many other puppets and live actors in Jabba's Palace.<ref name="Habidabad" />

During the scene when a captured [[Princess/Legends|Princess]] [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa]] is presented to Jabba after freeing [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]] from the [[Carbonite/Legends|carbonite]], Marquand told Philpott to try to get Jabba to really lick the side of Carrie Fisher's face in order to create a genuine disgusted reaction. Philpott resisted at first since the tongue, which was covered in [[Wikipedia:K-Y Jelly|K-Y Jelly]], was difficult to control, but Marquand eventually convinced him to do it. During the scene in which he made the attempt, Philpott accidentally stuck the tongue right into Fisher's ear, which caused a small commotion on the set, although Philpott was unaware of what he did from within the Jabba costume until he later took a break from filming.<ref name="Blue Harvest" />

The brief scene in which Jabba eats the frogs took several takes to get correctly since it was difficult for Philpott to reach Jabba's mouth with the left arm; the filmmakers originally tried to use an actual [[Wikipedia:Frog|frog]] but it kept escaping and hopping around the set.<ref name="T-Bone" /> The final scene Philpott filmed was the death of Jabba the Hutt; he and the other puppeteers "trashed around and went crazy" and Philpott said once Carrie Fisher realized she couldn't hurt the performers inside Jabba, "she really went for it."<ref name="T-Bone" /> Philpott described actor [[Harrison Ford]] as "rueful, and funny as he appeared;" actress [[Carrie Fisher]] as mischievous on the set and petulant due to being stuck in a long contract; and actor [[Mark Hamill]] was "a bit insecure" and not nearly as boyish as he seemed on-screen.<ref name="Blue Harvest" /> Like most bit players in the [[Original trilogy|original ''Star Wars'' trilogy]], Philpott was bought out of his contract in [[1983]], the year ''Return of the Jedi'' was released, and thus receives no future royalties for his participation in the film.<ref name="Yildiz">{{WebCite|url=http://www.yildizsavaslari.com/inceleme/tobyphilpott/index_english.html|text=Toby Philpott Interview|work=YildizsaVaslari.com|archivedate=20080917175238}}</ref>

===Later life===
[[File:Philpott_coppinger.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Toby Philpott and [[John Coppinger]], [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]] sculptor.]]
Philpott remains well known for his animatronics work, particularly on ''Return of the Jedi'', and attends occasional [[Wikipedia:Science fiction convention|science fiction conventions]] to meet fans and sign autographs,<ref name="T-Bone" /> including [[Celebration Europe (2007)|Celebration Europe]] in [[2007]].<ref name="Bothan Spy">{{WebCite|url=http://www.thebothanspy.com/interview.php?intid=8|text=Toby Philpott - Interview|date=2007-12-20|work=The Bothan Spy|archivedate=20080930210234}}</ref> Philpott said he had no idea how large the ''[[Star Wars]]'' fanbase was until he started using the [[Wikipedia:Internet|Internet]] in [[1999]].<ref name="Lightsabre" />

[[File:Philpott_jabbacrew.jpg|thumb|right|200px|[[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]] sculptor [[John Coppinger]] with Jabba [[Wikipedia:Puppeteers|puppeteers]] Mike Edmonds, David Barclay and Toby Philpott at Celebration Europe.]]
Following the [[1997]] [[The Star Wars Trilogy Special Edition|Star Wars Special Edition]] theatrical re-release of [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''A New Hope'']], Philpott said he did not like the new animated Jabba the Hutt featured in a previously-cut scene and, although he was interested in the future possibilities brought about by [[Wikipedia:Computer-generated imagery|computer-generated imagery]], he lamented the increased use of computer animations rather than animatronics and real performers.<ref name="Philpott Page" /> Philpott did not dismiss CGI altogether, especially when it appears seamless and convincing as he believed it did in [[Steven Spielberg]]'s [[1993]] film, [[Wikipedia:Jurassic Park (film)|''Jurassic Park'']]. However, he felt puppetry and animatronics often lent a more convincing look to the movies than the "slick hyper-reality" of some computer effects, and feels puppeteers have the unique ability to improvise and interact with the performers, unlike computer-generated images.<ref name="Tosche Station" /> Philpott would later admit, however, that the animated Jabbas that appeared in the [[2004]] [[Wikipedia:DVD|DVD]] re-release of ''Return of the Jedi'' and the 1999 [[Prequel trilogy|prequel]] [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''The Phantom Menace'']] were significant improvements over the special edition incarnation.<ref name="T-Bone" />
